text:
Self tinning computer paste would bond to it at a very low temperature while preventing direct oxidation, increasing the total thickness and resultant conductivity without harming the paper. With products like that you can both thicken, strengthen and oxygen-proof your final product! (~_^)-b

I assume though too, you can, once you have made your circuit, soak the paper fibers in a resin then electroplate more copper to it easily enough.

+Ivo Hulskamp
One idea may be to mix the copper nanoparticles with the epoxy in a vacuum chamber; of course this depends on how epoxy behaves in a vacuum, if it doesn't cure too fast or lose viscosity, this may work.

A less safe alternative may be to mix the epoxy and copper in an atmosphere devoid of oxygen, such as a pure Nitrogen atmosphere in a sealed chamber.

As long as the epoxy itself doesn't have any oxygen ions that are free to react with the copper, or the copper doesn't catalyze the curing of the epoxy, I would assume it would be Ok?

The only way to find out is to do an experiment.

author: @666sham1
text:
I bought some Aluminum fine powder (1-3 micron). It has no conductivity as a powder.

Aluminum Oxide powder (not as fine) on the other hand is conductive.

This leaves me wondering why the Aluminium powder does not conduct.

I can't be sure it is down to just oxidation alone.

replies.author: @neilruedlinger4851
replies.text:
+Schamim Schunna
The Aluminum nanoparticles may not have a crystal structure that allows close physical contact with their neighbors, preventing efficient conduction; at least that's my educated guess.

Does Aluminum powder have zero conductivity or nearly infinite electrical resistance, as measured by a multimeter or does it have a measurably high resistance?

replies.author: @666sham1
replies.text:
Neil Ruedlinger The fine Aluminium powders as with many of the other fine metallic powders register an open circuit on the multimeter
 
The only fine powder I have found which conducts well is graphite. 

There is a similar problem with magnetism using micron sized pieces of magnets. Some of the more efficient Rare Earth Magnets have known to combust when the grain size were reduced to a few microns.

replies.author: @neilruedlinger4851
replies.text:
+Schamim Schunna
I am suspicious of the purity of the Aluminum powder; I recall when I studied Materials Science at University, that it's not possible to have pure Al powder after exposure to the air because Al quickly reacts with Oxygen in the atmosphere, to form an oxide.

replies.author: @666sham1
replies.text: Yes that's right. However the large grain Aluminum Oxide powders are conductive though less conductive than non oxidised metal powders.

replies.author: @viridens
replies.text:
+Robert Murray-Smith  Soldering tests results so far as follows:
 The tracks were drawn on paper and card. I used 60:40 flux cored solder, and a temperature controlled iron. I tried both a high temperature fast-in-and-out technique, and a minimum melting temperature approach. Although tinning was possible in each case with care, the binding agent ( I used PVA) was heat damaged so that the soldered area and adjoining track became detached and unstable and so open circuit if disturbed. I countered this with a flood coat of superglue immediately after soldering with some success, but all a bit hit and miss.
I think the best approach is to pre-solder connecting wires to either a loop of thin wire or the head of a drawing pin which is then glued to the substrate, then paint on your tracks to contact these.

author: @robertcoyle1532
text:
i do electroforming and make a conductive surface by just painting on shellac and drizzling 300 mesh brass powder on the sticky surface.( doesn't oxidize as bad as copper powder) The advantage is, all the powder is on the surface, so it takes less. Also, you don't have mixed liquids that go bad, or the metal settles out.

Once the shellac has dried, you can brush any excess powder onto a paper sheet and add it back int the can... no waste. Just don't brush off the metal on the shellac.

Takes a little practice to zero in, but it works like a charm. To get an electrical contact on the conductive surface. I just use a piece of solid copper speaker wire to make a point contact... no soldering required.

author: @hfyaer
text:
Hi
Would it be possible to fuse the ink with High current? I mean the resistance of the ink is relativly high. So maybe by passing high current it will be able to reach copper fusing temperature. And I suppose at this point the copper will aglomerate, resistance will drop, and so will the temperature. In fact, I have tried this with a paste of copper i made. But I got the copper from electrolysis. I used flux as binding substance. The paste was conductive. But when i plug it to high current alternative voltage, the result was not welding. What happened was a rapid smoke ejection. And then a not conducting powder was left. In fact, it conducts a little when i press it. I suppose the flux evaporated rapidly and the copper oxidized. Do you have an idea on how to prevent that?
Thanks and cheers from France! great job!

replies.text: hfyaer If you attempt to fuse with current it will burn up your paper substrate.  You could paint on a plastic or wood substrate and then electroplate with nickle and completely eliminate the oxidization problem. You could also electroplate with copper first to obtain a uniform trace and fill in any micro gaps and then electroplate with nickle to eliminate any surface oxidization.    One of the electroplate tutorials  uses the same crystal Robert used here.  The plating technique is pretty easy and only needs a 3V power supply for the copper.

Aluminum is fairly reactive (can be used as a replacement for carbon when purifying iron, as demonstrated by Thermite) so if you decide to give it a try you should use only a small amount in an area where open flame won't be a problem at first, to see how it acts. Silver might, or might not, have the same problem. I think it would be more like copper than aluminum in this respect, but I'm honestly not certain.

replies.author: @roidroid
replies.text:
as21462
Here's an idea:  Use a 2 part glue (which i'll refer to as liquid A and B), and whatever spray gun you have.
- Coat the entire surface to be sprayed with liquid-A.
- Mix the copper nano-particles into liquid-B, put this into the spray gun and go at it.

This way you won't clog your spray gun with glue.  It will only react where it's been sprayed onto the surface.

text: Boiling them would just oxidise them.  Pour off the excess fluid and mix them with gum arabic let them dry naturally then burnish to a bright copper. Only prepare as much as you need. The particles are so small they will oxidise quickly and if you leave them uncovered for any length of time they will oxidise to red copper oxide then black. If they have gone red you can reduce them by using a xenon camera flash. If they are black they are ruined hope this helps

Nyle Steiner on his page sparkbangbuzz has done some stuff on sulphur-copper based memristors if you haven't seen it. The active materials seem to be sulphides and the structure seems to be of the MEM diode type where good ohmic contact of one electrode and a schottky barrier at the other electrode is important A look at printable diodes might be a way forward

author: @richfiles
text:
Of course, my primary concern is how easily aluminum oxidizes. I kinda expected that, hence why I've not proceeded further.

I don't yet know if any other metals work in place of the aluminum. The original discovery involved sulfur corroded brass, but the main reacting component was the copper. The original design was point contact, so I though of applying the ideas behind planar transistors to this problem, since that left point contact transistors obsolete.

I'd love to hear what you know.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: There are ways to do this - but, the problems with nanoparticles are that as the size decreases the volume to surface area ratio increases dramatically and therefore so does their reactivity in air. Some nanoparticles are so unstable as to be pyrophoric - like iron. Aluminium is so reactive when in nanoparticle formulation that the military have been looking at it as a possible bullet primer and rocket fuel. The commonly used precursor is aluminium hydride which is reduced in an oxalic acid

author: @richfiles
text: My theory, is if I can create an aluminum paint, then maybe I can apply a point to the copper/sulfur surface. If I seal around the pad with an insulating seal, leaving a small "hole" in the center, it both protects the pad, and provides the tiny point for the aluminum to make contact. I then paint a copper trace over the whole mess as my second "lead" of the component. Discrete memristor, on a PC board. Memristors gain or lose resistance depending on the polarity of current flow through them.

author: @richfiles
text: This was a very interesting and informative video. I wonder if there is a set of chemicals that can be used to precipitate aluminum and create a similar aluminum based paint. I'm trying to make memristors on copper. You expose a pad of copper to sulfur and allow it to react. You can apply an aluminum point contact to it and it forms a memristor, but I was wondering if there's a way to precipitate aluminum and make a paint of it. That could potentially make the component reasonably viable.
